Indicates the owner's participation status in operations and cost-bearing. Participating = actively bearing costs, Non-Participating = not bearing costs but retaining interest, Carried = costs borne by another party, Back-In = right to re-enter after payout, Farmed-Out = interest assigned to another party.
reference: Joint Operating Agreement (JOA) Standards, COPAS Guidelines

description: The date on which payout was achieved for this working interest, if applicable. Null if payout has not been reached or does not apply.

Indicates whether the working interest has reached payout (recovery of capital costs). Pre-Payout = costs not yet recovered, Post-Payout = costs recovered and interest may revert or change, Not-Applicable = payout provisions do not apply.

The method by which revenue is allocated to this working interest. Proportionate = revenue allocated by NRI decimal, Net = net of royalties and burdens, Gross = gross production share, Payout-Based = allocation changes after payout.
The date on which this working interest reverted or is scheduled to revert to another party, if applicable. Null if reversion has not occurred or does not apply.

Indicates whether this working interest is subject to reversion provisions (e.g., interest reverts to another party after payout or upon certain conditions). True = reversion applies, False = no reversion.

The condition that triggers reversion of this working interest, if applicable. Payout = reverts after payout, Time-Based = reverts after a specified date, Production-Based = reverts after cumulative production threshold, Event-Based = reverts upon a specific event.
The decimal fraction representing the landowner or mineral rights holder royalty interest burden on this working interest. Null if not applicable.
The name or identifier of the source system from which this working interest record originated (e.g., Quorum Land System, SAP JV Accounting). Used for data lineage and reconciliation.

Indicates whether revenue payments for this working interest are currently held in suspense due to title issues, missing documentation, or other administrative holds. True = in suspense, False = not in suspense.
Free-text description of the reason revenue payments are held in suspense for this working interest, if applicable. Null if not in suspense.
Free-text description of the conditions under which this working interest terminates, if applicable. May reference payout, production thresholds, time limits, or other contractual provisions.
Reference number or identifier for the title opinion document that validates the chain-of-title for this working interest. Used for legal and compliance verification.

The current status of the title for this working interest. Clear = title is clear and marketable, Defective = title defects identified, Curative-Pending = curative work in progress, Disputed = title is under dispute.
The decimal fraction representing the working interest ownership percentage. Represents the proportionate share of costs and gross production the owner is obligated to bear. Example: 0.25 = 25% WI.
reference: COPAS Accounting Guidelines, SEC Regulation S-X Rule 4-10

description: Date when the product was discontinued or phased out from the catalog. Null for active products. Used for product lifecycle management and historical reporting.

API gravity measurement of the refined product, indicating density relative to water. Higher values indicate lighter products. Measured in degrees API.
Total aromatics content as percentage by volume. Affects combustion characteristics and emissions. Regulated in many jurisdictions for environmental compliance.
Ash content as percentage by mass, representing inorganic residue after combustion. Important for fuel oils and indicates potential for deposit formation.
Benzene content as percentage by volume. Regulated for environmental and health reasons, particularly in gasoline products. EPA Tier 3 limits benzene to 0.62% annual average.

Boolean flag indicating whether this product is a blending component (True) used to create finished products, or a finished product itself (False). Blending components include reformate, alkylate, MTBE, ethanol.
Carbon residue (Conradson or Ramsbottom method) as percentage by mass, indicating tendency to form carbonaceous deposits. Relevant for heavier fuel products.
Cetane number for diesel products, measuring ignition quality and combustion performance. Higher values indicate better ignition characteristics. Typical range 40-60.
Cold Filter Plugging Point (CFPP) in degrees Celsius, the lowest temperature at which diesel fuel will pass through a standardized filter. Critical for cold weather operability.

business_glossary_term: American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) Color
description: ASTM color designation of the product (e.g., ASTM D1500 scale 0.5-8.0 for petroleum products, or Saybolt color for lighter products). Indicates product purity and quality.
Copper strip corrosion test result (e.g., 1a, 1b, 2a), indicating corrosiveness of the product to copper and copper alloys. Important for fuel system compatibility.
Timestamp when this product record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Density of the refined product measured in kilograms per cubic meter (kg/m³) at standard temperature (15°C). Used for volume-to-mass conversions and custody transfer.
Distillation temperature range in degrees Celsius (e.g., 150-250°C), indicating the boiling point distribution of hydrocarbon components. Defines volatility characteristics.
Date when this product specification became effective and available for commercial transactions. Used for managing specification changes and product transitions.
Excise duty or tax category code applicable to this product under relevant tax jurisdictions. Determines applicable excise rates and reporting requirements.

description: Date when this product specification expires or is superseded. Null for products with indefinite availability. Used for managing product lifecycle and specification transitions.
Classification indicating whether the product is sold domestically, exported internationally, or both. Affects pricing, taxation, and regulatory compliance requirements.
Flash point temperature in degrees Celsius, the lowest temperature at which vapors ignite when exposed to an ignition source. Critical safety and transportation classification parameter.
Freeze point temperature in degrees Celsius, particularly relevant for jet fuel. The temperature at which hydrocarbon crystals form, affecting fuel flow and filter plugging.
Harmonized System tariff classification code (6-10 digits) for international trade and customs purposes. Used for import/export documentation and duty calculation.
Hazard classification for transportation and storage (e.g., Class 3 Flammable Liquids). Determines handling, storage, and transportation requirements.
reference: PHMSA Hazardous Materials Regulations, IMDG Code

description: Net (lower) heating value of the product in megajoules per kilogram (MJ/kg), representing energy content. Used for energy balance calculations and pricing.
Timestamp when this product record was last modified. Used for change tracking and audit purposes.
Lubricity measurement in microns (wear scar diameter at 60°C), indicating lubricating properties of diesel fuel. Critical for fuel injection system protection, especially in ULSD.
Olefins content as percentage by volume. Influences gasoline stability and emissions. Subject to regulatory limits in some markets.
Oxygenate content (ethanol, MTBE, ETBE) as percentage by volume. Mandated in many jurisdictions for emissions reduction. Affects fuel properties and compatibility.

description: UN packing group classification (I=high danger, II=medium danger, III=low danger) indicating degree of hazard for transportation purposes.
reference: UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods

description: Pour point temperature in degrees Celsius, the lowest temperature at which the product will flow. Critical for cold weather operability and storage.
Specific grade or quality designation of the refined product (e.g., Premium, Regular, Ultra Low Sulfur, High Sulfur, Grade A-1, ISO 8217 RMG380). Defines quality tier within product type.

Current lifecycle status of the refined product in the catalog. ACTIVE=currently produced and sold, INACTIVE=temporarily not available, DISCONTINUED=permanently removed from catalog, PENDING_APPROVAL=awaiting regulatory or commercial approval, RESTRICTED=limited availability or special authorization required.
Research Octane Number rating for gasoline products, measuring resistance to engine knocking. Applicable to MOGAS and AVGAS products. Typical range 87-100.
Sediment and particulate content as percentage by mass. Indicates product cleanliness and potential for filter plugging and equipment wear.
Smoke point measurement in millimeters for jet fuel and kerosene, indicating burning quality and tendency to produce smoke. Higher values indicate cleaner combustion.
Sulfur content of the refined product measured in parts per million (ppm). Critical for environmental compliance and product classification (e.g., ULSD typically <15 ppm).

description: UN number for hazardous materials classification and transportation (e.g., UN1203 for gasoline, UN1202 for diesel). Required for shipping documentation and safety compliance.
reference: UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods

description: Reid Vapor Pressure (RVP) measured in kilopascals (kPa), indicating volatility and evaporation tendency. Critical for gasoline products and environmental compliance.
Kinematic viscosity of the product measured in centistokes (cSt) at a specified temperature. Indicates flow characteristics, particularly important for fuel oils and lubricants.
Water content measured in parts per million (ppm). Excessive water can cause corrosion, microbial growth, and operational issues. Critical quality parameter for all refined products.

business_glossary_term: Accidental Release - Containment and Cleanup Methods
description: Recommended methods and materials for containing and cleaning up spills or releases, including absorbent materials, recovery procedures, and disposal considerations.
Visual description of the products appearance including color, clarity, and physical form (e.g., clear amber liquid, white crystalline powder).
Minimum temperature at which the product will spontaneously ignite without an external ignition source, measured in degrees Celsius.
reference: ASTM E659 Standard Test Method for Autoignition Temperature

description: Environmental conditions that may cause hazardous reactions or decomposition, such as heat, sparks, static electricity, shock, or incompatible materials.
System timestamp indicating when this safety data sheet record was first created in the data management system.
Recommended disposal methods and regulatory requirements for the product and contaminated containers, including waste treatment options and applicable waste codes.
Current lifecycle status of the safety data sheet document indicating whether it is in draft, active use, superseded by a newer version, archived, or withdrawn.
Environmental impact data including aquatic toxicity, persistence and degradability, bioaccumulative potential, mobility in soil, and effects on wildlife.
Date from which this safety data sheet becomes the official document for the product, replacing any prior versions.
24-hour emergency contact telephone number for incidents involving the product, typically a poison control center or manufacturer emergency line.
Date when this safety data sheet expires and must be reviewed or replaced, typically required every 3-5 years depending on jurisdiction.

Workplace air concentration exposure limits including OSHA Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) and ACGIH Threshold Limit Value (TLV), expressed as time-weighted average (TWA), short-term exposure limit (STEL), or ceiling limit.

description: Appropriate fire extinguishing agents for fires involving the product (e.g., foam, CO2, dry chemical, water spray). Critical for emergency response planning.
Special fire and explosion hazards associated with the product, including toxic combustion products, explosive vapor formation, and reactivity with water.
Recommended first aid procedures for eye contact with the product, including flushing duration, removal of contact lenses, and when to seek medical attention.
Recommended first aid procedures if the product is swallowed, including whether to induce vomiting, aspiration hazards, and medical treatment guidance.
Recommended first aid procedures if the product is inhaled, including moving to fresh air, oxygen administration, and when to seek medical attention.
Recommended first aid procedures for skin contact with the product, including washing procedures, removal of contaminated clothing, and medical treatment guidance.
Lower explosive limit (LEL) or lower flammability limit, expressed as volume percent in air, below which the vapor-air mixture is too lean to ignite.

description: Upper explosive limit (UEL) or upper flammability limit, expressed as volume percent in air, above which the vapor-air mixture is too rich to ignite.
reference: ASTM E681 Standard Test Method for Concentration Limits of Flammability

description: Lowest temperature at which vapors of the product will ignite when exposed to an ignition source, measured in degrees Celsius. Critical for fire hazard assessment.
reference: ASTM D93 Standard Test Methods for Flash Point

description: Globally Harmonized System hazard classification for the product, including hazard classes and categories (e.g., Flammable Liquids Category 2, Acute Toxicity Category 4).
Safe handling practices and precautions to minimize exposure and prevent accidents, including grounding requirements, ventilation needs, and incompatible materials to avoid.
UN transport hazard class or division number indicating the primary hazard for transportation (e.g., Class 3 Flammable Liquids, Class 2.1 Flammable Gases).
reference: UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods

description: List of GHS hazard pictogram codes applicable to the product (e.g., GHS02 Flame, GHS07 Exclamation Mark, GHS08 Health Hazard), separated by semicolons.
Standardized GHS hazard statements describing the nature and degree of the hazards (e.g., H225 Highly flammable liquid and vapor, H304 May be fatal if swallowed and enters airways).
Hazardous substances that may be formed when the product decomposes or burns, such as carbon monoxide, hydrogen sulfide, sulfur oxides, or nitrogen oxides.
Materials and chemical classes that should not come into contact with the product due to risk of hazardous reactions, including strong oxidizers, acids, bases, or reactive metals.
Date when this version of the safety data sheet was officially issued or published by the responsible party.
Name of the organization or entity responsible for issuing and maintaining the safety data sheet, typically the manufacturer, importer, or distributor.
Three-letter ISO 639-2 language code indicating the language in which the safety data sheet is written (e.g., ENG for English, SPA for Spanish).
System timestamp indicating when this safety data sheet record was last updated or modified in the data management system.
Characteristic odor or smell of the product (e.g., petroleum-like, sweet, pungent, odorless), important for leak detection and worker awareness.
UN packing group indicating the degree of danger for transport: I (high danger), II (medium danger), III (low danger). Not applicable for all hazard classes.
reference: UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods

description: Physical state or form of the product at normal temperature and pressure (solid, liquid, gas, vapor, or aerosol).

description: Standardized GHS precautionary statements describing recommended measures to minimize or prevent adverse effects (e.g., P210 Keep away from heat/sparks/open flames, P280 Wear protective gloves/eye protection).

Version number or revision identifier of the safety data sheet, used to track updates and changes over time.
GHS signal word indicating the relative level of severity of hazard: Danger for more severe hazards, Warning for less severe hazards.
Description of the products solubility or miscibility in water (e.g., negligible, slightly soluble, miscible, insoluble), important for spill response and environmental impact.
Ratio of the density of the product to the density of water at a specified temperature. Values less than 1.0 indicate the product floats on water; greater than 1.0 indicates it sinks.

Summary of toxicological data including acute toxicity (LD50/LC50), routes of exposure, target organs, chronic health effects, carcinogenicity, and reproductive toxicity.
Four-digit United Nations identification number for dangerous goods used in international transport (e.g., UN1203 for gasoline, UN1075 for LPG).

description: Total aromatic hydrocarbon content as volume percent. Aromatics affect octane number in gasoline, cetane number in diesel, and smoke point in jet fuel.

description: Asphaltene content as weight percent. High asphaltene content affects crude stability, blending compatibility, and processing difficulty in heavy oil upgrading.

description: Current lifecycle status of the assay record. Validated and approved assays are used for refinery linear programming (LP) models and crude evaluation.
Conradson Carbon Residue as weight percent. Indicates coke-forming tendency of the crude, particularly important for vacuum residue processing and coking unit feed evaluation.
reference: ASTM D189 - Conradson Carbon Residue of Petroleum Products

description: Timestamp when this assay record was first created in the system. Part of audit trail for data lineage and compliance.
Volume percent yield of diesel fraction (520-650°F) from distillation. Diesel fraction is processed into automotive diesel, heating oil, and marine fuel.

description: Laboratory distillation method used to generate the yield curve. ASTM D86 is atmospheric batch distillation; TBP (True Boiling Point) uses ASTM D2892 (atmospheric) and D5236 (vacuum) for detailed fractionation.

description: Lowest temperature at which vapors above the crude will ignite when exposed to an ignition source. Critical safety parameter for storage and transportation classification.
reference: ASTM D93 - Flash Point by Pensky-Martens Closed Cup Tester

description: Gross heating value (higher heating value) in British Thermal Units per pound. Used for energy content calculations and fuel value assessment.
reference: ASTM D240 - Heat of Combustion of Liquid Hydrocarbon Fuels by Bomb Calorimeter

description: Hydrogen sulfide content in parts per million. H2S is highly toxic and corrosive. Crudes with >100 ppm H2S require special handling and safety protocols.

description: Hydrogen content as weight percent. Higher hydrogen content indicates more paraffinic character and better refining yields. Used in hydrogen balance calculations.
reference: ASTM D5291 - Instrumental Determination of Carbon, Hydrogen, and Nitrogen

description: Iron metal content in parts per million. Elevated iron indicates contamination from pipelines, storage tanks, or production equipment.
reference: ASTM D5863 - Determination of Nickel, Vanadium, Iron, and Sodium in Crude Oils

description: Volume percent yield of kerosene fraction (380-520°F) from distillation. Kerosene is used for jet fuel and heating oil production.
reference: ASTM D86 - Distillation of Petroleum Products at Atmospheric Pressure

description: Mercaptan sulfur content in parts per million. Mercaptans are odorous sulfur compounds that must be removed or converted in refining to meet product specifications.

description: Timestamp when this assay record was last modified. Tracks updates to assay data as new analyses are performed or corrections are made.
Volume percent yield of naphtha fraction (IBP-380°F) from ASTM D86 or True Boiling Point (TBP) distillation. Naphtha is feedstock for gasoline blending and petrochemical reforming.
reference: ASTM D86 - Distillation of Petroleum Products at Atmospheric Pressure

description: Nickel metal content in parts per million. Nickel and vanadium are catalyst poisons in FCC and hydrocracking units, concentrated in heavy residue fractions.
reference: ASTM D5863 - Determination of Nickel, Vanadium, Iron, and Sodium in Crude Oils

description: Total nitrogen content in parts per million. Nitrogen compounds poison catalysts in fluid catalytic cracking (FCC) and hydrotreating units.
reference: ASTM D4629 - Trace Nitrogen in Liquid Petroleum Hydrocarbons

description: Olefin (unsaturated hydrocarbon) content as volume percent. Olefins are typically low in crude oil but increase in thermally cracked products. Affect stability and gum formation.
reference: ASTM D1319 - Hydrocarbon Types in Liquid Petroleum Products by Fluorescent Indicator Adsorption

description: Lowest temperature at which the crude oil will pour or flow under prescribed conditions. Critical for pipeline transportation and storage in cold climates.
reference: ASTM D97 - Pour Point of Petroleum Products

description: Reid Vapor Pressure measured in pounds per square inch at 100°F. Indicates volatility and light-end content. Important for safety and environmental compliance.
reference: ASTM D323 - Vapor Pressure of Petroleum Products (Reid Method)

description: Volume percent yield of vacuum residue (1050°F+). Residue is processed in coking, visbreaking, or deasphalting units, or used as fuel oil or asphalt feedstock.
reference: ASTM D1160 - Distillation of Petroleum Products at Reduced Pressure

description: Salt content expressed as pounds per thousand barrels (PTB). High salt content causes corrosion in crude distillation unit (CDU) overhead systems and requires desalting.
reference: ASTM D3230 - Salts in Crude Oil (Electrometric Method)

description: Saturated hydrocarbon (paraffins and naphthenes) content as volume percent. Saturates are desirable for diesel cetane number and lube oil base stocks.
reference: ASTM D1319 - Hydrocarbon Types in Liquid Petroleum Products by Fluorescent Indicator Adsorption

description: Specific gravity of the crude relative to water at standard conditions (60°F/60°F). Used in volume-to-mass conversions and refinery yield calculations.
reference: ASTM D4052 - Density and Relative Density of Liquids

description: Total sulfur content of the whole crude expressed as weight percent. Critical for classifying crude as sweet (<0.5%) or sour (>0.5%) and determining hydrodesulfurization (HDS) requirements.
reference: ASTM D4294 - Sulfur in Petroleum Products by Energy Dispersive X-ray Fluorescence

business_glossary_term: Total Acid Number (TAN) in mg KOH/g
description: Total Acid Number representing naphthenic acid content, measured in milligrams of potassium hydroxide per gram of sample. High TAN crudes (>0.5) cause corrosion in refinery equipment.
reference: ASTM D664 - Acid Number of Petroleum Products by Potentiometric Titration

business_glossary_term: Universal Oil Products (UOP) K Characterization Factor
description: UOP K characterization factor calculated from boiling point and specific gravity. Indicates paraffinic (K>12), naphthenic (K=11-12), or aromatic (K<11) character of the crude.

The financial instrument or mechanism used to remit payment. Includes wire transfer, Automated Clearing House (ACH), check, letter of credit, netting arrangements for Joint Interest Billing (JIB), and electronic funds transfer (EFT).

Current lifecycle status of the payment. Received indicates initial recording, cleared indicates successful application, bounced indicates failed bank processing, reversed indicates cancellation, pending indicates awaiting clearance, and reconciled indicates bank statement matching completed.
The accounting period date on which the payment transaction was posted to the general ledger. May differ from payment_date due to period-end cutoffs and accounting calendar rules.
The profit center responsible for the revenue associated with this payment. Used for segment reporting and business unit performance analysis.

Status of bank reconciliation for this payment. Unreconciled indicates no bank statement match, reconciled indicates successful matching, exception indicates discrepancies requiring investigation, and pending_review indicates awaiting manual review.
External reference number or transaction identifier provided by the payer or payment processor. Used for reconciliation and traceability across banking systems.
Textual information provided by the payer detailing which invoices or accounts the payment is intended to settle. Critical for accurate cash application and dispute resolution.
The shortfall amount when the payment received is less than the invoice amount due, excluding authorized discounts. May indicate disputes or payment errors.
The acceptable variance threshold for payment matching. Small differences within tolerance are automatically cleared without manual intervention.
The portion of the payment amount that remains unallocated to specific invoices. May represent overpayments, advance payments, or payments pending manual allocation.
The effective date on which funds become available for use, as determined by the banking institution. May differ from payment_date due to clearing cycles.
The unique reference number assigned by the bank for wire transfer payments. Critical for tracing international crude oil and Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) payments.
Unique identifier for the accounts receivable record. Primary key for the receivable ledger entry.
reference: COPAS Accounting Guidelines, IFRS 9 Financial Instruments

Classification of the receivable based on days past due. Current = not yet due; 30/60/90/90+ days = days overdue. Used for aging reports, expected credit loss provisioning, and SOX compliance reporting.

description: Business identifier for the AR document. External reference number used in SAP FI-AR module for tracking and reconciliation.

reference: COPAS Accounting Guidelines, IFRS 15 Revenue from Contracts with Customers

description: Current state of the receivable in the collections workflow. Open = normal AR; in_collection = active collection efforts; payment_plan = customer on installment agreement; legal_referral = sent to legal counsel; written_off = deemed uncollectible; closed = fully paid.
reference: COPAS Credit and Collections Standards, IFRS 9 Derecognition

description: Cost center that originated the billable activity. Used for cost allocation and internal charging.

description: System timestamp when the receivable record was first created in the AR ledger. Used for audit trail and SOX compliance.

description: Payment terms agreed with the customer, expressed in standard notation (e.g., Net 30, 2/10 Net 30, Net 45). Defines the payment period and any early payment discounts.

description: Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for the receivable amount. Supports multi-currency operations for international crude oil, refined product, and petrochemical sales.

description: Number of days the receivable is overdue, calculated as current date minus due date. Negative values indicate payment is not yet due. Used for precise aging analysis and collection prioritization.

description: Date the customer formally raised the dispute. Used to track dispute aging and resolution cycle time.

description: Indicates whether the customer has disputed this receivable. True if the customer has raised a billing dispute; false otherwise. Disputed receivables are excluded from aggressive collection actions.
reference: COPAS Dispute Resolution Procedures, SAP FI-AR Dispute Management

description: Free-text description of the customer's dispute reason. Captures the nature of the billing disagreement for resolution tracking and root cause analysis.
reference: COPAS Dispute Resolution Procedures, SAP FI-AR Dispute Management

description: Date by which payment is contractually due. Calculated from invoice date plus credit terms. Used for aging bucket classification and dunning trigger.

description: Escalation level of collection notices sent to the customer. Typically ranges from 0 (no dunning) to 3+ (final notice before legal action). Increments with each dunning cycle.

description: Communication channel used for the last dunning notice. Tracks how collection notices are delivered to the customer.
reference: SAP FI-AR Dunning Configuration, COPAS Credit and Collections Standards

description: Provision amount for expected credit loss calculated per IFRS 9 ECL model. Based on probability of default, loss given default, and exposure at default. Used for financial reporting and reserve adequacy.
reference: IFRS 9 Expected Credit Loss Model, GAAP ASC 326 Current Expected Credit Losses

description: Fiscal period (month) within the fiscal year when the receivable was created. Typically 1-12 for monthly periods. Used for monthly financial close and reporting.

description: Fiscal year in which the receivable was created. Used for period-based financial reporting and year-over-year analysis.
reference: IFRS IAS 1 Presentation of Financial Statements, GAAP ASC 205 Presentation of Financial Statements

description: General ledger account code for the receivable. Maps to the chart of accounts for financial statement reporting and SOX compliance.
reference: IFRS IAS 1 Presentation of Financial Statements, GAAP ASC 210 Balance Sheet, SOX Section 404 Internal Controls

description: Date the most recent dunning notice was sent to the customer. Used to schedule the next dunning cycle and track collection activity.

description: System timestamp when the receivable record was last updated. Tracks changes to balance, status, or other attributes for audit trail and SOX compliance.
reference: SOX Section 404 Internal Controls, SAP FI-AR Audit Trail

description: Amount of the most recent payment applied to this receivable. Used for payment tracking and cash application analysis.
reference: SAP FI-AR Payment Processing, COPAS Accounting Guidelines

description: Date of the most recent payment applied to this receivable. Used to track payment activity and customer payment behavior.
reference: SAP FI-AR Payment Processing, COPAS Accounting Guidelines

description: Date the receivable was referred to legal counsel for collection action. Indicates escalation to litigation or formal legal demand.
reference: COPAS Credit and Collections Standards, SAP FI-AR Legal Collections

description: The original invoiced amount when the receivable was first created. Represents the total amount owed before any payments, credits, or adjustments.
reference: IFRS 9 Financial Instruments, GAAP ASC 606 Revenue Recognition

description: Current unpaid balance remaining on the receivable after applying payments, credits, and adjustments. Used for aging analysis and expected credit loss provisioning.
reference: IFRS 9 Expected Credit Loss Model, GAAP ASC 326 Current Expected Credit Losses

description: Indicates whether the customer is on an installment payment plan for this receivable. True if payment plan is active; false otherwise.
reference: COPAS Credit and Collections Standards, SAP FI-AR Payment Plans

description: Profit center responsible for the revenue and receivable. Used for internal management reporting and profitability analysis.
reference: SAP CO Profit Center Accounting, COPAS Accounting Guidelines

description: Date revenue was recognized for this receivable per IFRS 15 or GAAP ASC 606 revenue recognition standards. May differ from invoice date based on performance obligation satisfaction.

description: Amount of the receivable that has been written off as uncollectible. May be partial or full write-off. Impacts bad debt expense and expected credit loss reserves.

description: Date the receivable was written off. Used for financial reporting and audit trail. Write-offs must be approved per SOX internal control requirements.

description: Indicates whether this receivable has been written off as uncollectible. True if written off; false otherwise. Write-offs require management approval and are reported for SOX compliance.

description: Business justification for the write-off. Common reasons include customer bankruptcy, uncollectible after legal action, small balance administrative write-off, or disputed amount settlement.

description: Unique identifier for the credit note record. Primary key.

Current workflow status of the credit note. Tracks progression through approval gates required by SOX controls and revenue policy.

Free-text field for additional comments, instructions, or context related to the credit note. Supports customer communication and internal documentation.
Payment terms applicable to the credit note settlement (e.g., Net 30, Immediate, Applied to AR Balance). Supports cash application and accounts receivable management.

description: Date on which the credit note transaction was posted to the general ledger. May differ from credit_date due to period-end cutoffs or approval delays.

description: Indicates whether this credit note is a reissue or correction of a previously issued credit note. Supports audit trail and version control.

description: Total tax amount being reversed or adjusted by this credit note (e.g., sales tax, VAT, excise duty). Supports tax compliance and reconciliation.

description: Tax jurisdiction or authority under which the tax adjustment applies (e.g., US-Federal, UK-HMRC, TX-State). Supports multi-jurisdictional tax reporting.

Physical quantity adjustment (in barrels, cubic feet, or metric tons) that triggered the credit note, if applicable. Nullable for non-volume-related credits. Supports measurement reconciliation and custody transfer audits.

description: Unit of measure for the volume adjustment quantity. Common values: BBL (barrels), MCF (thousand cubic feet), MT (metric tons), GAL (gallons). Nullable if no volume adjustment applies.
User ID or system identifier of the person or process that created the credit note record. Supports audit trail and segregation of duties compliance.
Unique system-generated identifier for the invoice record. Primary key for the invoice entity.
Reference to the customer or counterparty being billed. May be an IOC (International Oil Company), NOC (National Oil Company), refiner, trader, industrial consumer, or joint venture partner.

Net adjustments applied to the invoice for pricing corrections, quality adjustments, demurrage charges, or other post-billing modifications. May be positive or negative.
The cumulative amount received from the customer against this invoice to date. Used to calculate outstanding balance and track partial payments.
The last date of the period covered by this invoice. Defines the temporal scope of the billed activities or deliveries.
The first date of the period covered by this invoice. For production sales, this is the start of the lifting or delivery period. For JIB invoices, this is the start of the cost accumulation period.

business_glossary_term: Council of Petroleum Accountants Societies (COPAS) Billing Method
description: The COPAS-compliant billing method used for JIB invoices. Detailed method provides line-by-line cost breakdowns. Summary method aggregates costs by category. Lump sum method bills a pre-agreed fixed amount. Applicable only for invoice_type = jib.
The date and time this invoice record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code in which the invoice is denominated (e.g., USD, EUR, GBP). All monetary amounts on this invoice are expressed in this currency.
Total discount applied to the invoice, including volume discounts, early payment discounts, or contractual rebates. Reduces the amount due.
The date the customer formally notified Oil Gas of the invoice dispute. Used to track dispute aging and resolution SLAs.
Free-text description of the reason the customer has disputed this invoice. Populated when invoice_status = disputed. Used for dispute resolution tracking and root cause analysis.
The date by which full payment is expected from the customer per the agreed payment terms. Used for aging analysis, late payment interest calculation, and credit management.
The date this invoice was posted to the general ledger for financial reporting. Used for period close and financial statement preparation.

The date the invoice was formally issued to the customer. This is the official billing date used for revenue recognition timing under IFRS 15 and ASC 606, and the starting point for payment term calculations.
Externally-visible unique invoice number assigned to the billing document for customer reference and payment reconciliation. Used in customer communications and payment remittance.

Current lifecycle state of the invoice in the accounts receivable workflow. Draft invoices are pending approval. Issued invoices are approved but not yet transmitted. Sent invoices have been delivered to the customer. Disputed invoices have customer challenges requiring resolution. Partially paid invoices have received partial payment. Paid invoices are fully settled. Cancelled invoices are voided. Written-off invoices are uncollectible and removed from active receivables. [ENUM-REF-CANDIDATE: draft|issued|sent|disputed|partially_paid|paid|cancelled|written_off — 8 candidates stripped; promote to reference product]

Classification of the invoice based on the nature of the billing transaction. Commercial invoices are for third-party crude oil, refined product, or petrochemical sales. JIB (Joint Interest Billing) invoices are for joint venture partner cost allocations per COPAS standards. Royalty invoices are for mineral rights owner payments. Tariff invoices are for pipeline transportation or storage fees. Intercompany invoices are for internal cross-entity transactions. Service invoices are for technical or operational services rendered.
The date and time this invoice record was last updated. Used for audit trail, change tracking, and data quality monitoring.
The remaining unpaid amount on the invoice, calculated as total_amount - amount_paid. Zero when invoice is fully paid.

The payment instrument or mechanism agreed for settling this invoice. Wire transfer and ACH are electronic funds transfers. Check is paper-based. Letter of credit is a bank guarantee instrument common in international trade. Offset is used in joint venture netting arrangements.

The adjustment (positive or negative) applied to the reference_price to arrive at the actual transaction price. Accounts for quality differences, location basis, and contractual premiums or discounts. Expressed in the same units as reference_price.

High-level classification of the primary product or service being billed. Crude oil includes WTI, Brent, and other crude grades. Type of carry arrangement: full (100% of costs carried), partial (percentage of costs carried), casing_point (carry until casing point decision), or production (carry until first production).
Timestamp when this carried interest record was first created in the system.
Total amount in base currency that has been carried by the carrier partner to date, including all costs funded on behalf of the carried party.
Total amount in base currency that has been recovered by the carrier partner to date through the agreed recovery mechanism.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for all monetary amounts in this carried interest arrangement.
Contractual provisions that apply if the carried party defaults on obligations or if the carrier fails to fund agreed costs.
Date when the carried interest arrangement becomes effective and cost carrying begins.
Date when the carried interest arrangement expires or the carry period ends. Nullable for open-ended arrangements.
Terms under which the carried party may forfeit their interest if they fail to meet obligations or if recovery conditions are not met.
Method of interest calculation on the outstanding carry balance: simple (interest on principal only), compound (interest on principal plus accrued interest), or none (no interest).
Annual interest rate applied to the outstanding carry balance if the carry agreement includes interest accrual. Nullable if no interest applies.
User identifier of the person who last modified this carried interest record.
Timestamp when this carried interest record was last modified in the system.
Free-form text field for additional comments, special terms, or clarifications regarding the carried interest arrangement.
Current outstanding balance in base currency that remains to be recovered by the carrier partner. Calculated as cumulative_carried_amount minus cumulative_recovered_amount.
Date when the carried interest was fully recovered and payout was achieved. Nullable until payout is complete.
Current payout status of the carry arrangement: pre_payout (no recovery started), in_payout (recovery in progress), post_payout (fully recovered), or suspended (recovery temporarily halted).

Method by which the carrier recovers carried costs: gross_revenue (from gross production revenue), net_revenue (from net revenue after royalties), production_volume (from carried party's production volumes), or cost_allocation (through future cost allocations).
Multiplier applied to carried costs for recovery calculation. For example, 2.0 means carrier recovers twice the carried amount. Default is 1.0 for dollar-for-dollar recovery.
Percentage of the carried party's revenue or production that is applied to recover the carried costs. For example, 50% means half of carried party's revenue goes to carrier until carry is recovered.
Net revenue interest percentage that reverts to the carrier partner after payout is achieved. Nullable if no reversion applies.
Working interest percentage that reverts to the carrier partner after payout is achieved. Nullable if no reversion applies.

description: Indicates whether the audit involves NORM (Naturally Occurring Radioactive Material) compliance, which is a specific regulatory concern in oil and gas production operations. NORM findings require specialized handling, disposal, and regulatory reporting under EPA and state radiation control programs.
reference: EPA NORM Guidance; NRC Regulations; State Radiation Control Programs; API RP 7H1 NORM Management

description: The aggregate result or conclusion of the audit as determined by the auditing authority. Drives escalation, enforcement action, and executive compliance reporting. A VIOLATION_ISSUED outcome triggers mandatory corrective action tracking and potential regulatory penalty assessment.
reference: BSEE Inspection Outcome Classification; EPA Audit Resolution Policy; OSHA Citation and Penalty Procedures; SOX Section 302/404 Deficiency Classification

description: Monetary penalty or fine assessed by the regulatory agency as a result of the audit findings, expressed in the reporting currency. Nullable when no financial penalty is assessed. Supports financial liability tracking, CAPEX/OPEX impact analysis, and SEC disclosure obligations for material regulatory penalties.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250.1400; EPA 40 CFR Part 19; OSHA 29 CFR 1903.15; PHMSA 49 CFR Part 190; SEC Regulation S-K Item 103; SOX Section 302

description: ISO 4217 three-letter currency code for the penalty amount (e.g., USD, GBP, EUR). Required for multinational operations where penalties may be assessed in local currency. Supports financial consolidation and IFRS/GAAP reporting.
reference: ISO 4217 Currency Codes; IFRS/GAAP Financial Reporting Standards; SEC Regulation S-X

description: Deadline by which the assessed regulatory penalty must be paid to the governing agency. Drives accounts payable scheduling and financial liability management. Nullable when no penalty is assessed.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250.1400; EPA 40 CFR Part 19; OSHA 29 CFR 1903.15; PHMSA 49 CFR Part 190

description: Reference number of the prior audit in which the same or related finding was previously identified. Used to establish repeat-finding linkage for regulatory enforcement escalation and trend analysis. Nullable for first-occurrence findings.
reference: BSEE Repeat Violation Policy; EPA Audit Resolution Policy; OSHA Repeat Citation Standards

description: Narrative description of the corrective action recommended by the auditor to remediate the identified finding. May be prescribed by the regulatory agency or proposed by the internal compliance team. Forms the basis for the corrective action plan (CAP) and regulatory response submissions.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250.193; EPA Audit Resolution Policy; OSHA 29 CFR 1903.19; SOX Section 404 Remediation; Enablon HSE Management — Corrective Action Module

description: Geographic or legal jurisdiction under which the audit is conducted (e.g., 'Federal — Gulf of Mexico OCS', 'State — Texas Railroad Commission', 'Federal — EPA Region 6', 'International — North Sea UKCS'). Determines applicable regulatory framework and reporting obligations for multinational operations.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250; BOEM 30 CFR Part 550; EPA Regional Authority; FERC 18 CFR; PHMSA 49 CFR

description: The specific regulatory program or rule set under which the audit is conducted (e.g., 'SEMS — Safety and Environmental Management System', 'LDAR — Leak Detection and Repair', 'Pipeline Integrity Management', 'SEC Reserves Disclosure', 'OPEC Quota Compliance', 'ESG Reporting'). Maps the audit to the governing compliance framework.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250 Subpart S (SEMS); EPA 40 CFR Part 63 (LDAR); PHMSA 49 CFR Part 195; SEC Regulation S-X Rule 4-10; OPEC Production Quota Framework; ISO 14001

description: Date on which the formal audit report was officially issued by the auditing authority or internal compliance team. Triggers response deadlines and corrective action plan submission timelines under applicable regulatory frameworks.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250.193; EPA Audit Resolution Policy; OSHA 29 CFR 1903.19; SOX Section 404

description: Deadline by which the company must submit a formal written response or corrective action plan to the auditing authority. Calculated from report_issued_date per applicable regulatory requirements. Drives compliance calendar management and escalation workflows.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250.193; EPA Audit Resolution Policy; OSHA 29 CFR 1903.19; PHMSA 49 CFR Part 190; FERC 18 CFR

description: Actual date on which the company submitted its formal written response or corrective action plan to the auditing authority. Compared against response_due_date to assess regulatory response timeliness and compliance with submission deadlines.
reference: BSEE 30 CFR Part 250.193; EPA Audit Resolution Policy; OSHA 29 CFR 1903.19; PHMSA 49 CFR Part 190

description: Name or identifier of the individual, team, or organizational unit accountable for implementing the corrective action for the finding. Typically the operations manager, HSE manager, or process owner for the audited facility or function.
reference: Enablon HSE Management — Responsibility Assignment; SOX Section 302 Management Certification; API RP 75 SEMS Responsibility Matrix

description: Unique identifier for the fixed asset record. Primary key for the fixed asset master data.

Total depreciation, depletion, and amortization accumulated since capitalization. Calculated per successful efforts or full cost method for oil and gas properties.

description: Unique 14-digit API well identifier assigned by regulatory authorities. Standard identifier for well assets used across industry for regulatory reporting and data exchange.

description: Detailed business description of the fixed asset including make, model, location, and operational purpose.
External business identifier for the fixed asset. Unique asset tag or registration number used across the enterprise for asset tracking and reporting.
Estimated present value of future plug and abandonment costs for oil and gas wells and facilities. Capitalized as part of asset cost and accreted over asset life per GAAP ASC 410.

Current lifecycle status of the fixed asset. Determines whether depreciation is active, asset is capitalized, or asset is subject to impairment testing. description: Detailed sub-classification within the asset class. Examples include producing wells, non-producing wells, gathering systems, processing plants, FPSO units, compressor stations, refineries, pipelines, drilling rigs, and support facilities.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA Custom Field, API Standards

description: Date when the asset was placed in service and capitalized on the balance sheet. Marks the start of depreciation and depletion calculations.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLA-AKTIV, IFRS IAS 16, GAAP ASC 360

description: Timestamp when the fixed asset record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Total production to date in depletion units. Used as numerator in unit of production depreciation calculation to determine depletion rate.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA Custom Field, GAAP ASC 932

description: Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for all monetary amounts. Used for multi-currency consolidation and foreign exchange translation.
Unit of measure for unit of production depreciation calculation. Typically BOE (Barrel of Oil Equivalent) for oil and gas properties, used to calculate depletion rate based on production volumes.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA Custom Field, GAAP ASC 932, SPE PRMS

description: Net book value in the book depreciation area for financial statement reporting per IFRS or GAAP.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLC Depreciation Area 01

business_glossary_term: Depreciation Area International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) Value
description: Net book value in the IFRS depreciation area for international financial reporting and consolidation.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLC Depreciation Area 30, IFRS IAS 16

description: Net book value in the tax depreciation area for tax return preparation and deferred tax calculation.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLC Depreciation Area 15

description: Depreciation method applied to the asset. Unit of production is standard for oil and gas producing properties; straight-line for facilities and equipment.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLA-AFASL, IFRS IAS 16, GAAP ASC 932

description: Total estimated recoverable reserves in depletion units for oil and gas producing properties. Used as denominator in unit of production depreciation calculation. Aligned with SEC proved reserves classification.
reference: SPE PRMS, SEC Regulation S-X Rule 4-10, GAAP ASC 932

description: Type of facility asset such as FPSO, platform, compressor station, processing plant, refinery unit, pipeline, or storage terminal. Used for asset classification and maintenance planning.
Geographic location of the asset including country, state/province, and basin. Used for regulatory reporting, tax jurisdiction determination, and portfolio segmentation.
Indicates whether the asset has been identified for impairment testing due to triggering events such as commodity price decline, reserves downgrade, or operational issues.
Cumulative impairment loss recognized when the carrying amount exceeds the recoverable amount. Required for SEC ceiling test compliance for oil and gas properties under full cost method.
reference: IFRS IAS 36, GAAP ASC 360, SEC Regulation S-X Rule 4-10

description: Date of the most recent depreciation calculation run. Used to track periodic depreciation posting and ensure timely financial close.
Date of the most recent impairment test performed on the asset. Required quarterly for full cost pool ceiling test and annually for successful efforts method.
Timestamp when the fixed asset record was last updated. Used for change tracking and SOX compliance audit trails.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLA-AEDAT, SOX Section 404

description: Current net book value of the fixed asset calculated as acquisition value minus accumulated depreciation. Represents the carrying amount on the balance sheet.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA Calculated Field, IFRS IAS 16

description: Ownership percentage representing the company's share of production revenue after royalties and overriding royalty interests. Used for revenue allocation and reserves valuation.
Indicates whether the company is the operator of the asset. Operator is responsible for day-to-day operations, AFE preparation, and joint interest billing.
Name of the operating company responsible for the asset if the company is non-operated. Used for joint venture partner tracking and JIB reconciliation.
Date when the asset was retired, disposed, or abandoned. Marks the end of depreciation and triggers gain/loss calculation.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLA-DEAKT, IFRS IAS 16

description: Cash or fair value received from asset sale or disposal. Used to calculate gain or loss on retirement.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA Custom Field, IFRS IAS 16

description: Type of asset retirement transaction. Determines accounting treatment for gain/loss recognition and plug and abandonment (P&A) cost capitalization.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLA-ANLHTXT, GAAP ASC 410

business_glossary_term: Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) Proved Property Classification
description: SEC reserves classification for oil and gas properties: PDP (Proved Developed Producing), PDNP (Proved Developed Non-Producing), PUD (Proved Undeveloped). Required for SEC financial disclosure and ceiling test compliance.
reference: SEC Regulation S-X Rule 4-10, SPE PRMS, GAAP ASC 932

description: Estimated useful life of the asset in years for straight-line or declining balance depreciation methods. Not applicable for unit of production method.
reference: SAP S/4HANA FI-AA ANLA-NDJAR, IFRS IAS 16

description: Ownership percentage representing the company's share of capital costs and operating expenses for the asset. Used for joint venture accounting and AFE cost allocation.

reference: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets, ASC 360 Property Plant and Equipment

description: Foreign key linking to asset.asset_facility. Business justification: Impairment assessments are performed on facility-level CGUs (cash-generating units) per IAS 36/ASC 360. impairment_assessment has fixed_asset_id but not asset_facility_id — facilities are the operatio

Foreign key linking to finance.company_code. Business justification: Impairment assessments are performed for legal entities. The existing company_code STRING attribute should be replaced with a proper FK to company_code.company_code_id to enable legal entity-level imp
Reference to the cost center associated with the asset or CGU being assessed for impairment.

Foreign key linking to finance.fixed_asset. Business justification: Impairment assessments are conducted on specific fixed assets (proved properties, wells, pipelines, facilities) to determine if their carrying amount exceeds recoverable amount. The impairment_assessm
Reference to the general ledger account to which the impairment loss or reversal is posted.
Reference to the profit center responsible for the asset or CGU being assessed for impairment.

Foreign key linking to finance.wbs_element. Business justification: Impairment assessments in oil and gas are conducted at the cash-generating unit (CGU) level, which often corresponds to WBS elements representing specific fields, wells, or development projects. The i

Foreign key linking to asset.well_asset. Business justification: Well-level impairment assessments (ceiling test, proved property impairment) are standard in oil and gas under ASC 932 and IFRS 6. No existing FK from impairment_assessment to well_asset — critical fo
The oil and gas accounting method used: successful efforts method or full cost method, which determines how exploration and development costs are capitalized and amortized.
reference: SEC Regulation S-X Rule 4-10, IFRS 6 Exploration for and Evaluation of Mineral Resources

description: The accounting standard framework applied for the impairment assessment: IAS 36 (International Accounting Standard), ASC 360 (Accounting Standards Codification), IFRS (International Financial Reporting Standards), or GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles).
The date on which the impairment assessment was formally approved by the reviewer or management.
The date on which the impairment assessment was performed or completed.
Business-facing unique reference number assigned to the impairment assessment for tracking and reporting purposes.
reference: IFRS/GAAP Oil and Gas Accounting Standards

description: The current lifecycle status of the impairment assessment: draft, in review, approved, posted to the general ledger, or cancelled.
Classification of the impairment assessment based on the reason or timing of the evaluation.
The assumed Brent crude oil price per barrel used in the impairment assessment cash flow projections.
The book value or carrying value of the asset or CGU on the balance sheet at the assessment date, before any impairment loss is recognized.
The calculated ceiling test limit under the full cost method, representing the maximum allowable capitalized costs based on the present value of estimated future net revenues from proved reserves.
Indicates whether a ceiling test was performed as part of the impairment assessment under the full cost method, which limits capitalized costs to the present value of estimated future net revenues.
The username or identifier of the user who created this impairment assessment record in the system.
The timestamp when this impairment assessment record was first created in the system.
The three-letter ISO 4217 currency code in which all monetary amounts in this impairment assessment are denominated (e.g., USD, GBP, EUR).
The pre-tax discount rate applied in the discounted cash flow (DCF) calculation to determine value in use, reflecting the time value of money and asset-specific risks.
The Estimated Ultimate Recovery (EUR) of hydrocarbons from the asset over its entire productive life, used in the impairment assessment.

business_glossary_term: Fair Value Less Costs to Sell
description: The estimated amount obtainable from the sale of the asset in an arm's length transaction between knowledgeable, willing parties, less the costs of disposal.
reference: IAS 36 Impairment of Assets, IFRS 13 Fair Value Measurement

description: The fiscal period (month or quarter) within the fiscal year in which the impairment assessment was performed and recorded.
The fiscal year in which the impairment assessment was performed and recorded.
The assumed natural gas price per unit (e.g., per MMBTU or MCF) used in the impairment assessment cash flow projections.
The amount by which the carrying amount exceeds the recoverable amount, recognized as an impairment loss in the financial statements.
The amount of previously recognized impairment loss that is reversed in the current period due to an increase in the recoverable amount (not applicable to goodwill under IFRS).
The username or identifier of the user who last modified or updated this impairment assessment record in the system.
The timestamp when this impairment assessment record was last modified or updated in the system.
Additional notes, comments, or explanations related to the impairment assessment, including assumptions, methodologies, or special considerations.
The date on which the impairment loss or reversal was posted to the general ledger and reflected in the financial statements.
The commodity price forecast or price deck used in the impairment assessment, including references to benchmark prices such as West Texas Intermediate (WTI), Brent, or Henry Hub.

Classification of the oil and gas property being assessed: Proved Developed Producing (PDP), Proved Developed Non-Producing (PDNP), Proved Undeveloped (PUD), unproved properties, goodwill, or other assets.
The higher of the asset's fair value less costs to sell and its value in use, representing the maximum amount recoverable from the asset.
The name of the individual or team responsible for reviewing and approving the impairment assessment.
Indicates whether this impairment assessment is subject to Sarbanes-Oxley (SOX) internal control testing and compliance requirements.

Description of the event or indicator that triggered the impairment assessment, such as commodity price decline, reserve revision, regulatory change, abandonment decision, or adverse market conditions.
The date on which the triggering event occurred that necessitated the impairment assessment.
The present value of the future cash flows expected to be derived from the asset or CGU, calculated using discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis.
The assumed West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil price per barrel used in the impairment assessment cash flow projections.
Unique identifier for the tax provision record. Primary key for the tax provision entity.
reference: IAS 12 Income Taxes, ASC 740 Income Taxes

description: Foreign key linking to finance.company_code. Business justification: Tax provisions are calculated for legal entities. The existing company_code STRING attribute should be replaced with a proper FK to company_code.company_code_id to enable legal entity-level tax report

Foreign key linking to finance.gl_account. Business justification: Tax provisions are posted to specific GL accounts in the chart of accounts (current tax expense, deferred tax asset, deferred tax liability accounts). The tax_provision table tracks current_tax_expens

Foreign key linking to finance.profit_center. Business justification: Tax provisions in oil and gas multinational operations are calculated at the profit center (business segment) level for segment tax reporting, transfer pricing documentation, and country-by-country re
The accounting standard under which the tax provision is prepared. IFRS follows IAS 12, US GAAP follows ASC 740, and local GAAP follows jurisdiction-specific standards.
reference: IAS 12 Income Taxes, ASC 740 Income Taxes

description: Status of tax authority audit for this jurisdiction and period. Tracks whether the tax position is under examination by tax authorities (IRS, state revenue departments, international tax authorities).
The financial statement carrying amount (book basis) of the underlying asset or liability. The difference between book basis and tax basis creates the temporary difference.
The date on which the tax loss carryforward or tax credit carryforward expires if not utilized. Some jurisdictions allow indefinite carryforwards; others have time limits (e.g., 20 years).
Timestamp when the tax provision record was first created in the system. Used for audit trail and data lineage tracking.
Three-letter ISO 4217 currency code for all monetary amounts in this tax provision record (e.g., USD, CAD, GBP, EUR). All amounts are recorded in this currency.
The amount of income tax expense for the current period based on taxable income. Represents taxes payable or refundable for the current year.

The amount of income taxes recoverable in future periods related to deductible temporary differences, tax loss carryforwards, and tax credit carryforwards. Common in oil and gas for Depreciation Depletion and Amortization (DD&A) timing differences and Abandoned and Dry Hole (ADH) costs.
The amount of income tax expense or benefit related to changes in deferred tax assets and liabilities. Represents the tax effect of temporary differences between book and tax basis.

The amount of income taxes payable in future periods related to taxable temporary differences. Common in oil and gas for accelerated tax depreciation versus book depreciation and successful efforts versus full cost accounting differences.

The actual tax rate calculated as total tax expense divided by pretax book income. Expressed as a decimal. Differs from statutory rate due to permanent differences, rate changes, and jurisdictional mix. Key metric for SEC financial disclosure and investor analysis.
reference: IAS 12 Income Taxes paragraph 81, ASC 740-10-50-12, SEC Regulation S-K Item 303

description: Exchange rate used to convert foreign currency tax provisions to the reporting currency. Represents the rate from the local jurisdiction currency to the company reporting currency.
reference: IAS 21 The Effects of Changes in Foreign Exchange Rates, ASC 830

description: The fiscal period (month) within the fiscal year for which the tax provision applies. Typically 1-12 for monthly periods or 1-4 for quarterly periods.
reference: IAS 1 Presentation of Financial Statements, GAAP

description: The fiscal year for which the tax provision applies. Four-digit year representation.
reference: IAS 1 Presentation of Financial Statements, GAAP

description: Interest accrued on underpayment or overpayment of taxes, or on uncertain tax positions. Calculated based on applicable jurisdiction interest rates.
Code identifying the tax jurisdiction (federal, state, country, or local). Format: ISO country code for federal (e.g., USA, CAN, GBR), or country-state for state/provincial (e.g., USA-TX, CAN-AB).
reference: ISO 3166-1 alpha-3, IRS Publication 515, OECD Tax Guidelines

description: Full name of the tax jurisdiction (e.g., United States Federal, Texas, Alberta, United Kingdom).
Classification of the tax jurisdiction level: federal (national), state (US state), provincial (Canadian province), country (international), local (county/city), or municipal.
reference: IRS Tax Code, OECD Tax Guidelines

description: Timestamp when the tax provision record was last modified. Used for audit trail and change tracking to support SOX compliance.

Net deferred tax asset or liability position, calculated as deferred tax asset minus deferred tax liability. Positive values indicate a net asset position; negative values indicate a net liability position.
Penalties assessed or accrued related to tax positions, underpayments, or late filings. Common in oil and gas for royalty and severance tax disputes.

Differences between book income and taxable income that will never reverse. Examples in oil and gas include percentage depletion in excess of cost depletion, non-deductible fines and penalties, and tax-exempt income.
Income before income taxes per the financial statements (book income). Used as the starting point for the tax provision calculation and effective tax rate reconciliation.

Method used to calculate the tax provision. Actual method uses actual year-to-date results, estimated method uses projections, and annualized method estimates full-year effective tax rate applied to interim periods per IAS 34 and ASC 740-270.

Free-text notes documenting assumptions, judgments, and significant items in the tax provision calculation. Used to document complex positions such as transfer pricing, Production Sharing Agreement (PSA) interpretations, and uncertain tax positions.

Current status of the tax provision record in the financial close process. Draft provisions are prepared during the close cycle, preliminary provisions are subject to review, final provisions are approved for financial reporting, amended provisions reflect corrections, and audited provisions have been reviewed by external auditors.
Name of the individual who reviewed and approved the tax provision calculation. Required for SOX internal controls over financial reporting.

The date on which the statute of limitations expires for this tax period, after which the tax authority can no longer assess additional tax. Typically 3 years from filing date in the US, but varies by jurisdiction.
The enacted tax rate for the jurisdiction. Expressed as a decimal (e.g., 0.2100 for 21%). Used to calculate the expected tax expense and for effective tax rate reconciliation.

The tax basis of the underlying asset or liability that gives rise to the temporary difference. For oil and gas properties, represents the remaining tax basis for depletion and depreciation purposes under either successful efforts or full cost method.

Tax credits available to reduce current tax liability. Oil and gas specific credits include Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) credits, marginal well credits, Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) credits (Section 45Q), and renewable energy credits.
reference: IRS Tax Code Section 43 (EOR), Section 45Q (CCS), ASC 740-10-25-46

description: Net Operating Loss (NOL) available to offset future taxable income. Common in oil and gas during commodity price downturns or for companies with significant exploration and development expenditures.
reference: IRS Tax Code Section 172, IAS 12 Income Taxes paragraph 34, ASC 740-10-30-18

description: The date on which the tax return for this jurisdiction and period was filed with the tax authority. Used to track filing compliance and statute of limitations.
reference: IRS Tax Code Section 6072, SOX Section 404

description: Type of tax for which the provision is recorded. Oil and gas specific types include production tax (tax on volume produced), severance tax (state tax on extraction), windfall profit tax (tax on excess profits), and resource rent tax (tax on economic rent from resources).
reference: IRS Tax Code Section 29, State Severance Tax Statutes, OECD BEPS Guidelines

description: Income subject to tax as determined under the applicable tax law. Differs from book income due to permanent and temporary differences. For oil and gas, includes adjustments for Intangible Drilling Costs (IDC), depletion methods, and production-based deductions.
reference: IRS Tax Code Section 61, IAS 12 Income Taxes paragraph 5

description: Differences between the tax basis and book basis of assets and liabilities that will reverse over time. Common oil and gas temporary differences include DD&A timing, successful efforts versus tax accounting for exploration costs, and Asset Retirement Obligation (ARO) timing.
Total income tax expense for the period, calculated as current tax expense plus deferred tax expense. This is the amount reported on the income statement.

The amount of tax benefit from an uncertain tax position that does not meet the more-likely-than-not recognition threshold. Common in oil and gas for transfer pricing positions, Production Sharing Agreement (PSA) interpretations, and cross-border transactions.

Reduction in deferred tax assets when it is more likely than not that some portion or all of the deferred tax asset will not be realized. Common in oil and gas during commodity price downturns or for marginal properties.

Boolean flag indicating whether this actual cost represents a depreciation, depletion, or amortization charge. True for DD&A postings, False otherwise.
The fiscal period (month) within the fiscal year when the actual cost was posted. Typically ranges from 1 to 12 for regular periods, with special periods for year-end adjustments.
